rows.title: Brazil Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD): Federal Personal Data Protection Law
rows.summary:
Brazil's Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais, Law No. 13,709 of 14 August 2018 (LGPD), is the federal statute governing the processing of personal data of natural persons located in Brazil, enacted by the National Congress and subsequently amended by Law No. 13,853 of 8 July 2019, which also established the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD) as the supervisory authority.

On 14 August 2018, the National Congress enacted Law No. 13,709, with the majority of its provisions entering into force on 18 September 2020 following a series of postponements, and the administrative sanctions provisions entering into force on 1 August 2021. The law draws on principles established in earlier Brazilian data protection instruments, including the Marco Civil da Internet (Law No. 12,965/2014), and aligns with internationally recognised frameworks, including the European General Data Protection Regulation.

The LGPD establishes, under Article 1, rules for the processing of personal data carried out by natural persons or legal entities of public or private law, where the processing takes place in Brazil, where the purpose is to offer or supply goods or services to individuals located in Brazil, or where the personal data were collected in Brazil. Article 5 sets out definitions, including personal data, sensitive personal data (which encompasses data on racial or ethnic origin, religious belief, political opinion, trade union membership, health or sex life, genetic or biometric data), data subject, controller, operator and data protection officer. The law enumerates ten legal bases for processing under Article 7, including consent, legitimate interest, performance of a contract, and compliance with a legal obligation.

The ANPD is empowered under Articles 55-A through 55-L to issue regulations, conduct investigations, apply sanctions and coordinate with other regulatory bodies. Administrative sanctions provided under Article 52 include warnings, fines of up to 2% of the legal entity's revenue in Brazil in its last financial year, limited to a total of BRL 50,000,000 (approximately EUR 9,000,000 or USD 10,000,000) per infraction, daily fines, publication of the infraction, blocking or deletion of the personal data concerned, and partial or total suspension of database-related activities. Data subjects are granted rights under Articles 17 to 22, including rights of access, correction, anonymisation, portability, deletion, and information regarding entities with which data have been shared.

rows.title: Brazil Enacts Law No. 15,211/2025 Amending Child and Adolescent Statute to Address Digital Environment Protections
rows.summary:
Brazil has enacted Law No. 15,211/2025, known as the ECA Digital, amending the Estatuto da Criança e do Adolescente (ECA, Law No. 8,069/1990) to introduce protections for children and adolescents in digital environments, including provisions governing social media platforms, online content, and data processing practices directed at minors.

On 6 March 2025, the Brazilian President signed Law No. 15,211/2025 into law following its passage by the Congresso Nacional. The instrument amends and supplements the ECA, which has served as Brazil's principal framework for the rights and protections of persons under eighteen years of age since 1990, extending that framework explicitly to digital contexts.

The Law establishes obligations for providers of social networks, applications, and digital platforms operating in Brazil with respect to users identified as children, defined as persons under twelve years of age, and adolescents, defined as persons aged twelve to seventeen. Among the measures the Law provides are requirements for age verification mechanisms, restrictions on algorithmic amplification of content considered harmful to the psychological or physical development of minors, and prohibitions on targeted advertising directed at children. The instrument also addresses the processing of personal data of minors in digital environments, operating in conjunction with the Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais (LGPD, Law No. 13,709/2018) and guidance issued by the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD). Platforms found in breach of the provisions face administrative sanctions, with fines reaching up to BRL 50,000,000 (approximately EUR 8,400,000 or USD 9,100,000) per infraction, in addition to the possibility of suspension of activities within Brazilian territory.

The Congresso Nacional determined that enforcement responsibilities are distributed across multiple bodies, including the ANPD for data-related matters and the Ministério dos Direitos Humanos e da Cidadania for broader child-rights compliance. The Law sets a transitional period of one hundred and eighty days from publication for platforms to adapt their systems and policies to meet the new requirements set out in the instrument.

rows.title: Brazil Enacts ECA Digital Regulations, Imposing New Obligations on Platforms Serving Children and Adolescents
rows.summary:
Brazil's federal government has regulated Lei nº 15.211/2025, known as the Estatuto Digital da Criança e do Adolescente (ECA Digital), establishing a framework of obligations for digital platforms, online services and virtual environments accessible to children and adolescents, with enforcement responsibility assigned to the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD).

On 18 March 2026, at a ceremony at the Palácio do Planalto in Brasília, President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva signed three decrees implementing the new law. The first decree regulates the ECA Digital itself. The second creates the Centro Nacional de Proteção à Criança e ao Adolescente within the Federal Police, centralising reporting mechanisms and expanding the state's capacity to respond to digital crimes. The third strengthens the ANPD, designating it as the authority responsible for regulating, supervising and ensuring compliance with the new rules. The government also announced the allocation of BRL 100 million (approximately EUR 17 million or USD 18 million) through Finep to support the development of technological solutions for child protection in digital environments.

The Secretaria de Governo Digital sets out that the ECA Digital establishes obligations for applications, games, social networks and other digital services operating in Brazil. Among the measures the legislation introduces are the replacement of self-declaration with reliable age verification systems, the prompt removal of illegal content, and the adoption of default safety configurations for accounts held by minors. The law also addresses practices the government identifies as harmful to younger users, including manipulative design intended to encourage compulsive platform use and loot box mechanics associated with gambling-like reward systems. The legislation further regulates the commercial activities of child influencers, requiring judicial authorisation for commercial content, and strengthens parental supervision instruments.

The Ministra da Gestão e da Inovação em Serviços Públicos, Esther Dweck, noted that the decree provides for the public sector to act not only as regulator and supervisor but also as a promoter of implementation solutions. The ministry states it is committed to working alongside state-owned technology companies Serpro and Dataprev to develop public technological solutions supporting ECA Digital implementation. The ANPD's reinforced mandate, the government states, encompasses data protection oversight and the definition of technical parameters for the law's application, extending the existing framework of Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados to the specific context of minors in digital environments.

rows.title: Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation Publishes Updated Overview of National AI Strategy Established Under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617
rows.summary:
The Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation has published an updated overview of the Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial, the national artificial intelligence strategy established by Portaria MCTI nº 4.617 of 6 April 2021 and amended by Portaria MCTI nº 4.979 of 13 July 2021, setting out the framework's objectives, governance structure and nine thematic pillars.

On 23 September 2025, the Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation published the updated page for the Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial, referred to as the EBIA. The Ministry states that the strategy was instituted under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617 and subsequently amended, with no financial penalties attached to the instrument.

The Ministry states that the EBIA is aligned with the OECD principles endorsed by Brazil and is founded on five core principles for responsible AI governance: inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being; human-centred values and equity; transparency and explicability; robustness, safety and security; and accountability. The Ministry notes that the strategy was developed through three stages, comprising engagement of specialist consultancy, national and international benchmarking, and a public consultation conducted between 12 December 2019 and 3 March 2020 via a federal government electronic platform, which the Ministry states received approximately 1,000 contributions that informed the final document.

The Ministry states that the EBIA establishes nine thematic axes structured across two categories. The Ministry identifies three cross-cutting axes covering legislation, regulation and ethical use; AI governance; and international aspects. Six vertical axes address qualifications for a digital future; workforce and capacity building; research, development, innovation and entrepreneurship; application in productive sectors; application in the public sector; and public security. The Ministry states that under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617, the Ministry is required to create governance instances to prioritise, implement, monitor and update the strategic actions defined in the EBIA; coordinate actions enabling its implementation; invite public, private and academic institutions to support strategic actions; prepare evaluation reports on implementation; and assess the periodicity for updating the strategy. The Ministry states that governance of the EBIA is composed of the MCTI itself, the Rede MCTI/EMBRAPII de Tecnologias e Inovação Digital, and invited institutions.
rows.body_name: Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation
